planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination; seeds require cold stratification which fall planting provides naturally — meadow parsnip self-seeds reliably in moist woodland gardens and can also be divided in early spring.

directions: Meadow parsnip is a native woodland wildflower of the carrot family (Apiaceae) closely resembling golden alexanders (Zizia aurea) but with distinctly three-parted lower leaves and wider habitat tolerance — it grows in moist to mesic woodland openings, stream banks, and shaded meadow edges where filtered light is available. Flat-topped clusters of tiny golden-yellow flowers appear in late spring and are critically important early-season food for short-tongued native bees, parasitic wasps, soldier beetles, and early butterflies; it is also a documented larval host plant for the black swallowtail butterfly, making it doubly valuable in the native pollinator garden. The delicate, fernlike foliage remains attractive through the season in shade gardens, providing fine texture among coarser companions like wild ginger and hostas. Combine with Virginia bluebells, native columbine, and other spring ephemerals in a moist woodland garden for layered bloom from late winter through summer.
sources: North Carolina State Extension Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Plant container-grown vines in fall or early spring in average to moist, well-drained soil near a trellis, fence, or shrub to climb; yellow honeysuckle tolerates partial shade and can be trained as a scrambling groundcover or trained vertically for hummingbird gardens.

directions: Yellow honeysuckle is a native semi-evergreen vine of the Ozarks and Appalachian foothills, one of the most ornamentally beautiful of all American honeysuckles — its showy clusters of vivid golden-yellow to orange-yellow trumpet flowers in spring are magnets for ruby-throated hummingbirds during their northward migration and are also visited by long-tongued bumblebees and sphinx moths. Unlike the invasive Japanese honeysuckle (Lonicera japonica) that has escaped across the South, yellow honeysuckle is a well-behaved native that twines moderately and stays in bounds without smothering trees or shrubs; it is semi-evergreen in mild winters in the South. Small red berries ripen in summer and are eaten by mockingbirds and other fruit-eating birds. Train on a picket fence, stone wall, arbor, or through a native shrub border — it blooms most profusely in partial shade to full sun and combines beautifully with coral honeysuckle for extended bloom.
sources: North Carolina State Extension Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in fall in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately wet soil in partial to full shade; supplejack is a woody vine that forms thickets and climbs high into the forest canopy — site it where it has room to naturalize or provide a large, sturdy arbor.

directions: Supplejack is a vigorous native woody vine of bottomland forests, swamp margins, and moist woodland edges across the Gulf Coast and southeastern United States, climbing high into the forest canopy by twining and branching through the upper shrub and tree layers. Its flexible, wiry stems gave rise to the common name — the wood was historically used to make walking sticks, basket frames, and tool handles because of its exceptional suppleness and strength. Small, inconspicuous greenish-white flowers in late spring produce blue-black drupes that ripen in fall and are consumed by wood ducks, wild turkeys, and at least a dozen species of songbirds and small mammals, making supplejack one of the highest-value wildlife vines in the region. In naturalized woodland gardens or large-scale habitat restorations, allow it to climb through native trees and form dense thickets that provide both food and nesting cover; it is too vigorous for small spaces but invaluable for restoring native forest understory structure.

planting.note: Plant with crown at or slightly below soil level in moist, well-drained, slightly alkaline soil in partial shade; follow the classic clematis planting advice — head in the sun, feet in the shade — with cool mulch over the root zone.

directions: Leather flower is a graceful native clematis from the moist woodlands and streambanks of the central and eastern United States, distinguished by its nodding, urn-shaped flowers of leathery, thick-textured red-purple to mauve petals — the unusual closed, pitcher-like floral form is adapted for specialist bee pollinators that force their way inside for nectar, preventing nectar robbing by smaller insects. Unlike many showy cultivated clematis, leather flower blooms from late spring through summer with a steady succession of individual flowers rather than one dramatic flush, keeping the vine attractive for months; the feathery, plumed seedheads that follow add ornamental value into fall and winter. Grow on a fence, through a native shrub, or along a trellis in the partial shade of a woodland edge; pair with native ferns, columbines, and spicebush for a layered understory planting. Cut back to 12 inches in late winter before new growth resumes.

planting.note: Plant near a trellis or fence in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; swamp leather flower is one of the most flood-tolerant native clematis, naturally occurring along streambanks, swamp margins, and bottomland forest edges.

directions: Swamp leather flower is a native clematis of the Gulf Coastal Plain, blooming from late spring through summer with distinctive nodding, urn-shaped flowers in soft lavender to blue-purple with flared, recurved petal tips that give the flowers a crisp, sculptural quality unlike any other vine — it is one of the finest ornamental native vines of the American South yet remains underutilized in cultivation. The vine is naturally found in swampy thickets, bottomland forests, and moist shrub borders from Virginia to Texas, making it ideally suited to rain garden edges, pond margins, and low-lying moist areas of Gulf Coast gardens that challenge other vines. Unlike many Clematis species, it handles the heat and humidity of the Gulf South reliably without the mildew and root rot problems that affect hybrid large-flowered types in zones 8 and 9. Plant through a native shrub or on a fence in partial shade; cut back hard to the ground each winter to maintain a neat, vigorous plant.

planting.note: Plant in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade near a fence, trellis, or shrubby thicket; virgin's bower scrambles through shrubs and climbs by leaf petioles — site where it can naturalize freely as it self-seeds prolifically.

directions: Virgin's bower is the most widespread and cold-hardy of all native North American clematis, scrambling through shrubs and small trees along streams, roadsides, and woodland edges from Nova Scotia to Florida — in late summer it smothers its support in masses of small, four-petaled white flowers that are intensely fragrant and attract a spectacular diversity of native bees, wasps, beetles, and butterflies seeking late-summer nectar. After bloom, the flowers are replaced by feathery, silvery seed plumes that persist through fall and winter, creating a spectacular 'old man's beard' effect in the landscape — a three-season ornamental vine of understated elegance. While not as showy as large-flowered hybrid clematis, its fine-textured floral masses and subsequent silky seedheads provide months of changing interest with zero maintenance; it is drought-tolerant once established and pest-free. Self-seeds freely to form spreading colonies — allow it to naturalize in native shrub borders or along a fence where it can sprawl without becoming an obstacle.
sources: North Carolina State Extension Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Plant in moist, acidic, organically rich, well-drained to moist soil in full to partial shade; yellow anise tree performs best in the filtered shade of tall pines or oaks and adapts to wet to seasonally dry conditions across the Gulf Coastal Plain.

directions: Yellow anise tree is a rare Florida native evergreen shrub endemic to the Ocala National Forest and surrounding central Florida scrub and flatwoods, closely related to Florida anise (Illicium floridanum) but producing small, inconspicuous yellow flowers rather than red ones — its primary appeal is its dense, lustrous evergreen foliage and exceptional shade tolerance that make it one of the finest screening shrubs for difficult deep-shade sites in Gulf Coast landscapes. All parts of the plant contain toxic compounds similar to true star anise (Illicium verum) and emit a strong, pleasant anise-like fragrance when crushed, making deer browsing rare — an excellent choice where deer pressure limits other evergreen shrubs. Plants grow moderately quickly into dense, mounding 5–10 foot specimens that provide excellent year-round privacy screening in shaded settings where hollies and other broadleaf evergreens might struggle; they tolerate periodic flooding and are well-suited to moist, organic soils. As a critically rare Florida endemic, yellow anise tree deserves wider use in horticulture throughout zones 7–10 wherever a tough, fragrant, shade-tolerant evergreen screening shrub is needed.

planting.note: Plant with crown at soil level in well-drained, limestone-based or neutral to slightly alkaline soil in partial shade to full sun; Pitcher's clematis is a prairie-edge species naturally found on limestone bluffs, rocky slopes, and thicket edges in the south-central US.

directions: Pitcher's clematis is a native vine of the south-central United States — Texas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, and adjacent states — producing nodding, pitcher- or urn-shaped flowers of purplish-blue to violet-blue with thick, recurved petal tips from May through August on a twining vine that scrambles through shrubs and fences with a restrained, manageable habit. Its distinctive flowers are adapted to specialist bee pollinators and are particularly visited by native metallic green sweat bees that navigate the closed floral tube; the feathery, silvery seedheads that follow persist into winter as the seed plumes expand and catch the light. As a species adapted to drier, alkaline limestone soils, Pitcher's clematis is among the most heat- and drought-tolerant of all native clematis, performing well in zones 7–9 where summer heat and occasional drought challenge other species in the genus. Cut stems back to 12–18 inches in late winter to encourage vigorous new growth and prolific flowering the following season.

planting.note: Plant in moist to wet, acidic, organically rich soil in partial to full shade near a trellis, fence, or tree; climbing dogbane is a bottomland native that tolerates brief flooding and performs best in the shade of bottomland hardwood forests along Gulf Coast waterways.

directions: Climbing dogbane is a rare native alternative to the widely planted Confederate jasmine (Trachelospermum jasminoides), naturally found in the moist bottomland forests, swamp margins, and stream banks of the Gulf Coast from Virginia to Texas — it is the only species in its genus native to North America. Unlike its Asian relative, climbing dogbane adapts to shaded conditions under bottomland oaks and cypress, making it one of the few woody ornamental vines that will thrive in genuine shade; it produces clusters of small, fragrant, yellowish-white five-petaled flowers in early summer that are visited by native bees and butterflies. The milky latex sap makes it unpalatable to deer, and the plant is host to several specialist milkweed butterflies and moths in the family Apocynaceae. While less commonly available commercially than Confederate jasmine, climbing dogbane is worth seeking out from native plant nurseries for shaded trellises, arbors, and woodland garden structures where a fragrant, native evergreen vine is needed.

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in moist, rich soil near a trellis or at the margin of a pond, stream, or rain garden; climbing hemp weed is a fast-growing perennial vine in zones 8 and warmer, dies back after hard frost but returns reliably from the roots in Gulf Coast gardens.

directions: Climbing hemp weed is a vigorous native twining vine of the aster family (Asteraceae), native to wetland margins, pond edges, freshwater tidal marshes, and moist thickets across the Gulf Coastal Plain and eastern United States — despite being little-known in cultivation, it is one of the highest-value native vines for late-season pollinators. Dense clusters of small, fluffy white to pinkish flowers cover the vine from late summer through fall and are magnets for migrating monarchs and a remarkable diversity of native specialist bees; it is among the most important native late-season nectar sources in Gulf Coast wetland habitats. The vine climbs and twines quickly in warm seasons, making it useful for rapidly covering trellises, fences, and chain-link at the edges of rain gardens, constructed wetlands, and moist riparian restoration plantings. In zone 9 and south it remains semi-evergreen; in zone 8 it freezes to the ground but returns vigorously from the roots each spring.
